title: Charmed Life - Chapter 16 (Fragment 3)
text:
“About the only thing left is running away,” said Cat.

“Then let’s do that—at once,” said Janet.

They did not exactly run. They walked briskly out of the village on the road Cat thought pointed nearest to Wolvercote. When Janet objected that Wolvercote was the first place anyone at the Castle would think of looking, Cat explained about Mrs. Sharp’s grand contacts in London. He knew Mrs. Sharp would smuggle them away somewhere, and no questions asked. He made himself very homesick by talking of Mrs. Sharp. He missed her dreadfully. He trudged along the country road, wishing it was Coven Street and wishing Janet was not walking beside him making objections.

“Well, you may be right,” Janet said, “and I don’t know where else we could go. How do we get to Wolvercote? Hitchhike?” When Cat did not understand, she explained that it meant getting lifts by waving your thumb.

“That would save a lot of walking,” Cat agreed.

The road he had chosen shortly turned into a very country lane, rutted and grassy and lined with high hedges hung with red bryony berries. There was no traffic of any kind.

Janet managed not to point this out. “One thing,” she said. “If we’re going to make a proper go of this, do promise me you won’t happen to mention You Know Who.” When Cat did not understand this either, she explained, “The man Mr. Nostrum kept calling That Person and the Master of the Castle—you know!”

“Oh,” said Cat. “You mean Chrest—”

“Quiet!” bawled Janet. “I do mean him, and you mustn’t say it. He’s an enchanter and he comes when you call him, stupid! Just think of the way that Mr. Nostrum was scared stiff to say his name.”

Cat thought about this. Gloomy and homesick as he was, he was not anxious to agree with anything Janet said. She was not really his sister, after all. Besides, Mr. Nostrum had not been telling the truth. And Gwendolen had never said Chrestomanci was an enchanter. She would surely never have dared do all the magic she did if she thought he was. “I don’t believe you,” he said.

“All right. Don’t,” said Janet. “Just don’t say his name.”

“I don’t mind,” said Cat. “I hope I never see him again anyway.”

The lane grew wilder as they walked. It was a crisp, warm afternoon. There were nuts in the hedges and great bushes of blackberries. Before they had gone another half mile, Cat found his feelings had changed entirely. He was free. His troubles had been left behind. He and Janet picked the nuts, which were just ripe enough to eat, and laughed a good deal over cracking them. Janet took her hat off—as she told Cat repeatedly, she hated hats—and they filled the crown of it with blackberries for later on. They laughed when the juice oozed through the hat and dripped down Janet’s dress.

“I think running away is fun,” said Cat.

“Wait till we’re spending the night in a rat-infested barn,” said Janet. “Flitterings and squeakings. Are there ghouls and goblins in this wor—? Oh, look! There’s a car coming! Thumb—no, wave. They probably don’t understand thumbing.”

They waved furiously at the big black car that was whispering and bouncing along the ruts towards them. To their delight, it sighed to a stop beside them. The nearest window rolled down. They got a very rude shock when Julia put her head out of it.

Julia was pale and agitated. “Oh please come back!” she said. “I know you ran away because of me, and I’m sorry ! I swear I won’t do it anymore!”

Roger put his head out of the back window. “I kept telling her you would,” he said. “And she didn’t believe me. Do come back. Please.”

The driver’s door had opened by then. Millie came hurrying around the long bonnet of the car. She looked much more homely than usual, because her skirts were looped up for driving and she was wearing stout shoes and an old hat. She was as agitated as Julia. When she reached Janet and Cat, she flung an arm around each of them and hugged them so hard and thankfully that Cat nearly fell over.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling or appearing troubled or nervous.
vocabulary.example: Julia was pale and agitated.

vocabulary.definition: Experiencing worry, unease, or nervousness, typically about an imminent event or something with an uncertain outcome.
vocabulary.example: He was not anxious to agree with anything Janet said.

vocabulary.definition: The hinged cover over the engine of a motor vehicle.
vocabulary.example: She came hurrying around the long bonnet of the car.

vocabulary.definition: In an active, quick, or energetic way.
vocabulary.example: They walked briskly out of the village.

vocabulary.definition: A type of climbing plant with red berries, often poisonous.
vocabulary.example: Hedges hung with red bryony berries.

vocabulary.definition: Firm, dry, and brittle, especially in a way considered fresh and pleasing.
vocabulary.example: It was a crisp, warm afternoon.

vocabulary.definition: To a very great degree; extremely.
vocabulary.example: He missed her dreadfully.

vocabulary.definition: A person who uses magic; a sorcerer or magician.
vocabulary.example: He’s an enchanter and he comes when you call him.

vocabulary.definition: Made something clear or easy to understand.
vocabulary.example: Cat explained about Mrs. Sharp’s grand contacts in London.

vocabulary.definition: Light, quick movements or fluttering sounds, often used to describe small creatures moving about.
vocabulary.example: Flitterings and squeakings.

vocabulary.definition: Thrown or cast with force or carelessly.
vocabulary.example: She flung an arm around each of them.

vocabulary.definition: In a very angry, fast, or energetic manner.
vocabulary.example: They waved furiously at the big black car.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling or looking dark or poorly lit, especially to seem depressing or solemn.
vocabulary.example: Gloomy and homesick as he was...

vocabulary.definition: To travel by getting free rides in passing vehicles, usually by signalling with the thumb.
vocabulary.example: How do we get to Wolvercote? Hitchhike?

vocabulary.definition: Unattractive in appearance.
vocabulary.example: She looked much more homely than usual.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling unhappy because one is away from home.
vocabulary.example: He made himself very homesick by talking of Mrs. Sharp.

vocabulary.definition: Held tightly in one's arms, especially to express affection.
vocabulary.example: She hugged them so hard and thankfully.

vocabulary.definition: Moving with haste.
vocabulary.example: Millie came hurrying around the long bonnet of the car.

vocabulary.definition: Overrun or swarming with unwanted organisms such as pests or vermin.
vocabulary.example: Spending the night in a rat-infested barn.

vocabulary.definition: Folded or gathered and secured, usually fabric, temporarily or for convenience.
vocabulary.example: Her skirts were looped up for driving.

vocabulary.definition: To refer to something briefly or casually in speech or writing.
vocabulary.example: Do promise me you won’t happen to mention You Know Who.

vocabulary.definition: Expressed or felt disapproval, opposition, or dissent.
vocabulary.example: Janet objected that Wolvercote was the first place anyone at the Castle would think of looking.

vocabulary.definition: Marked by deep tracks or grooves made by wheels.
vocabulary.example: The road had turned into a very country lane, rutted and grassy.

vocabulary.definition: To move (goods or people) illegally into or out of a country.
vocabulary.example: Mrs. Sharp would smuggle them away somewhere, and no questions asked.

vocabulary.definition: High-pitched, short, sharp sounds, often from small animals or rodents.
vocabulary.example: Flitterings and squeakings.

vocabulary.definition: Strong, thick, and sturdy in build.
vocabulary.example: She was wearing stout shoes.

vocabulary.definition: To make a solemn statement or promise undertaking that one will do something or that something is true.
vocabulary.example: I swear I won’t do it anymore!

vocabulary.definition: Walked slowly and with heavy steps, typically because of exhaustion or harsh conditions.
vocabulary.example: He trudged along the country road.
